pages.full_text:
The glacier appeared to have created a giant elevated causeway on its SE flank as the sun melted that side and forced it to drop boulders and moraine like a huge dam that then kept the glacier clinging to the side of the valley.
Simon had lunch at Zamboni in blinding sunlight. Occasionally he glimpsed M. Rosa through clouds towering impossibly above. There were many hanging glaciers high up the mountain.
 After lunch, he climbed up the side moraine to a cairn where a Dutch woman was seated. When he took out his camera she said they had forgotten to put the SD card so had no pictures of their mountain adventure so Simon took a few of her family and wrote his email address in her iPhone so that later she could send her email address and he could send her the photos.
pages.route_sketch_description: Hand-drawn diagram at top of page showing a mountain landscape. Labels include 'Sun' (top centre), 'Monte Rosa' (top right), 'footpath' (centre, with arrow pointing to glacier), 'glacier' (centre), 'Zamboni' (left, with arrow), and 'open river' (bottom left). Arrows indicate the path and relationship between features.
pages.notes: The sketch at the top of the page is a diagram/map of the glacier area around Zamboni and Monte Rosa. The label 'M. Rosa' in the text likely refers to Monte Rosa. The word 'causeway' could possibly read 'carroway' but 'causeway' fits context best.

pages.full_text: Very twisty roads on the way back to the campsite. We stopped at Bogognna where there was an old castle built to keep the swiss away from Lake Maggiore. Then
photo_captions: View of Monterosa (2nd highest mtn of Europe) from Amo[illegible] near Lake d'Orta
pages.notes: The page contains a large glued-in photograph of a mountain landscape. The photo caption is written vertically along the right edge: 'View of Monterosa (2nd highest mtn of Europe) from Amo[?] near Lake d'Orta' — the word after 'from' is partially obscured by the page edge; could be 'Amora', 'Arona', or similar. The place name 'Bogognna' may be 'Borgogna' or 'Borgomanero' — transcribed as written. The text ends mid-sentence ('Then') indicating the narrative continues on the next page. A partial page number may be visible at top right but is not clearly legible.
